{"action":"opened","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/LinearAlgebra.jl/issues/645","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/LinearAlgebra.jl","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/LinearAlgebra.jl/issues/645/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/LinearAlgebra.jl/issues/645/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/LinearAlgebra.jl/issues/645/events","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/LinearAlgebra.jl/issues/645","id":2694351088,"node_id":"MDU6SXNzdWUyNjk0MzUxMDg4","number":645,"title":"More accurate result for matrix acos(A)","user":{"login":"jarlebring","id":11163595,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjExMTYzNTk1","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/11163595?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/jarlebring","html_url":"https://github.com/jarlebring","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jarlebring/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jarlebring/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jarlebring/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jarlebring/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jarlebring/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jarlebring/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jarlebring/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jarlebring/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jarlebring/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":5,"created_at":"2019-07-29T05:47:23Z","updated_at":"2024-11-26T11:34:03Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","sub_issues_summary":{"total":0,"completed":0,"percent_completed":0},"active_lock_reason":null,"body":"The matrix `acos(A)`-function for real triangular `A` returns a results which could be more accurate: 
```julia
julia> A=[0.1 0 ; 3.0 0.2]
2×2 Array{Float64,2}:
 0.1  0.0
 3.0  0.2
julia> acos(A)
2×2 Array{Complex{Float64},2}:
  1.47063-1.12757e-17im      0.0+0.0im        
 -3.03571-6.78237e-17im  1.36944+3.81639e-17im
```
Correct result (with Schur-Parlett method):
```julia
julia> [acos(A[1,1]) 0 ; A[2,1]*(acos(A[1,1])-acos(A[2,2]))/(A[1,1]-A[2,2]) acos(A[2,2])]
2×2 Array{Float64,2}:
  1.47063  0.0    
 -3.03571  1.36944
```
The non-zero imaginary part, is within machine precision in relative sense considering complex round-off, but a zero imaginary part would be better. (Yes, it does cause some problems for me.)

The reason the `acos(A)` returns a non-zero imaginary part is because it uses an identity which does not preserve realness under round-off. Illustration for scalars: 
```julia
julia> s=0.1;
julia> -im *log(s+im*sqrt(1-s^2))
1.4706289056333368 - 1.1275702593849246e-17im
julia> acos(s)
1.4706289056333368
```
","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/LinearAlgebra.jl/issues/645/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/LinearAlgebra.jl/issues/645/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null}}

Currently only `zero` does so:

```julia
julia> b=BigFloat(0.3,precision=100);
julia> b.prec
100
julia> o=one(b)
1.0
julia> o.prec
256
julia> z=zero(b)
0.0
julia> b.prec
100
```","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/56042/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/56042/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null}}

They do not. 

```julia
julia> b=BigFloat(0.3,precision=100);
julia> b.prec
100
julia> o=one(b)
1.0
julia> o.prec
256
julia> z=zero(b)
0.0
julia> z.prec
256
```","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/56042/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/56042/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/2399640217","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/56042#issuecomment-2399640217","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/56042","id":2399640217,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM6PB5qZ","user":{"login":"jarlebring","id":11163595,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjExMTYzNTk1","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/11163595?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/jarlebring","html_url":"https://github.com/jarlebring","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jarlebring/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jarlebring/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jarlebring/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jarlebring/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jarlebring/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jarlebring/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jarlebring/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jarlebring/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jarlebring/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-10-08T11:56:12Z","updated_at":"2024-10-08T11:56:12Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"In fact, I would also expect `oftype` to do the same. 
```
julia> b.prec
100
julia> q=oftype(b,0.1);
julia> b.prec
100
```
Note that `oftype` is used in `zero(x)` but not `one`:

https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/blob/4cdd864e535b16e928c2eff43e6f1583bd77209d/base/number.jl#L313

so changing `oftype` would solve it for `zero` but not for `one`.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/2399640217/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

* [ ] An implementation of *A. H. Al-Mohy and N. J. Higham, A new scaling and squaring algorithm
   for the matrix exponential, SIAM J. Matrix Anal. Appl., 31(3),  (2009)* This is basically the same as Higham 2005, but with a more accurate backward error estimate which in practice leads to less multiplications. The method behind `expm` in recent versions of matlab. 
* [ ] expmpoly: http://personales.upv.es/~jorsasma/software/expmpol.m described in *Boosting the computation of the matrix exponential, Appl. Math. Comput., 2018*  Requires only multiplications - no inverse. Main computation code can be generated with `graph_exp_sid` in [`GraphMatFun.jl`](https://github.com/matrixfunctions/GraphMatFun.jl).
* [ ] `ExpMethodArbitraryPrecision`. Arbitrary precision scaling-and-squaring:  https://epubs.siam.org/doi/10.1137/18M1228876?mobileUi=0","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/ExponentialUtilities.jl/issues/69/reactions","total_count":1,"+1":1,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/ExponentialUtilities.jl/issues/69/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/ExponentialUtilities.jl/issues/comments/2399703865","html_url":"https://github.com/SciML/ExponentialUtilities.jl/issues/69#issuecomment-2399703865","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/SciML/ExponentialUtilities.jl/issues/69","id":2399703865,"node_id":"IC_kwDOCJGC9s6PCJM5","user":{"login":"jarlebring","id":11163595,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjExMTYzNTk1","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/11163595?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/jarlebring","html_url":"https://github.com/jarlebring","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jarlebring/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jarlebring/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jarlebring/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jarlebring/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jarlebring/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jarlebring/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jarlebring/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jarlebring/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jarlebring/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-10-08T12:25:53Z","updated_at":"2024-10-08T12:25:53Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"The methods in this setting are to compute the martix `exp(A)`. The algorithm `expmv` is for `exp(matrix)v` where `v` is a vector (okay it can also be a tall skinny matrix). expmv belongs to a different method class, where you cannot use the squaring tricks we use in scaling-and-squaring.  An implementation of `expmv` in SciML would probably be appreciated, but probably fit better in a setting independent of the above methods. ```julia
julia> b=BigFloat(0.3,precision=100);
julia> b.prec

julia> o=one(b)
1.0

julia> z.prec
256
```","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/56042/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/56042/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/2400125199","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/56042#issuecomment-2400125199","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/56042","id":2400125199,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM6PDwEP","user":{"login":"jarlebring","id":11163595,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjExMTYzNTk1","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/11163595?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/jarlebring","html_url":"https://github.com/jarlebring","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jarlebring/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jarlebring/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jarlebring/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jarlebring/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jarlebring/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jarlebring/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jarlebring/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jarlebring/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jarlebring/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-10-08T15:10:42Z","updated_at":"2024-10-08T15:10:42Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"> Precision is not propagated, but rather always taken from the global (or scoped value) precision specified for doing that operation

I understand this now. If every operation leads to a new BigFloat with the global precision, it effectively means one cannot work with different precisions. For me, it sounds like julia does not support multiprecision with BigFloat. julia> o=one(b)

```","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/56042/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/56042/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":"completed"},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/comments/2400417139","html_url":"https://github.com/JuliaLang/julia/issues/56042#issuecomment-2400417139","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/JuliaLang/julia/issues/56042","id":2400417139,"node_id":"IC_kwDOABkWpM6PE3Vz","user":{"login":"jarlebring","id":11163595,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjExMTYzNTk1","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/11163595?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/jarlebring","html_url":"https://github.com/jarlebring","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jarlebring/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jarlebring/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jarlebring/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jarlebring/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jarlebring/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jarlebring/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jarlebring/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jarlebring/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/jarlebring/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-10-08T17:16:24Z","updated_at":"2024-10-08T17:16:24Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"Okay maybe I exaggerated a bit that it is completely impossible.

From a system design perspective I don't see why e.g sqrt(a) couldn't return an object the precision of `a` rather than a global precision. In the GMP-library the precision to be used for the computation is determined by the precision of destination variable, not the precision of the operation, and afaiu there is no global precision. 

Some things sound difficult to implement with this mental model. Suppose I have want to run ArnolidMethod.jl. I want to use one particular precision for the orthogonalization and basis matrix storage, and a different for the precision for the matrix vector product (implemented as a function or just a Matrix{BigFloat}). I don't see how that can be done without actually modifying the ArnoldiMethod.jl-code.
